cobo.original_spanish: Hay en estas sierras un árbol llamado de los indios quina-quina, de cuya corteza se hace el polvo que tanto renombre ha ganado en Europa para las tercianas y cuartanas. Es árbol mediano, de madera dura y pesada, con hojas parecidas al laurel pero más grandes y de color más oscuro. Los indios lo usaban desde tiempo inmemorial para las calenturas que llaman ellos chuccho, que son las que nosotros llamamos tercianas.
cobo.translation: In these mountains there is a tree called by the Indians quina-quina, from whose bark is made the powder that has gained such renown in Europe for tertian and quartan fevers. It is a medium-sized tree of hard and heavy wood, with leaves resembling the laurel but larger and of darker color. The Indians had used it from time immemorial for the fevers they call chuccho, which are what we call tertian fevers.
cobo.preparation: Bark ground to powder, dissolved in wine or water
cobo.notes: Cobo personally transported cinchona bark to Europe in 1632. This is the source of quinine, the first effective antimalarial drug.

modern_research.pubmed_summary: Quinine isolated 1820. Still first-line treatment for severe malaria. Quinidine used for cardiac arrhythmia. WHO Essential Medicine.
modern_research.significance: One of the most consequential pharmacological discoveries in history. Transformed treatment of malaria globally.

cobo.original_spanish: La hoja de coca es una de las más preciosas cosas que tiene este reino del Perú, y la que más estiman los indios, así los de la sierra como los de los llanos. Es un arbusto de vara y media de alto poco más o menos, con hojas pequeñas y verdes. Los indios la mastican mezclándola con cal o con cierta tierra blanca que llaman llipta, sacando della grande vigor y sustento, de manera que caminan días enteros sin comer ni beber, ni sentir cansancio. Úsanla también para el dolor de muelas y cabeza, aplicándola sobre la parte dolorida.
cobo.translation: The coca leaf is one of the most precious things in this kingdom of Peru, and that which the Indians esteem most, both those of the mountains and those of the lowlands. It is a shrub of about a vara and a half in height, with small green leaves. The Indians chew it mixed with lime or with a certain white earth they call llipta, drawing from it great vigor and sustenance, so that they walk entire days without eating or drinking, nor feeling fatigue. They also use it for toothache and headache, applying it to the painful area.
cobo.preparation: Leaves chewed with alkaline substance (lime or llipta) to release alkaloids; applied topically for pain
cobo.condition: Altitude sickness, fatigue, hunger suppression, dental and head pain, anesthesia
cobo.notes: Cobo's description of topical anesthetic use predates cocaine's isolation by two centuries. The alkaline co-administration he describes is pharmacologically sophisticated — necessary for alkaloid absorption.

modern_research.pubmed_summary: Cocaine first local anesthetic (1884). All subsequent local anesthetics (novocaine, lidocaine) derive from cocaine's mechanism. Coca leaf whole-plant research shows cardiovascular and metabolic benefits distinct from isolated cocaine.
modern_research.significance: Transformed surgery. The topical pain application Cobo documents is precisely the mechanism of local anesthesia.

cobo.original_spanish: La muña es hierba pequeña y muy olorosa que se cría en las tierras frías de la sierra, de hojas menudas y flor blanca. Tiénenla los indios en gran estima para los males de estómago y para ayudar a digerir. También la usan para el mal de altura que da a los que suben a los cerros nevados, que les causa gran fatiga y desmayo. Dícese asimismo que fortifica los huesos y es remedio para los que tienen los huesos blandos.
cobo.translation: Muña is a small and very fragrant herb that grows in the cold highland regions, with small leaves and white flowers. The Indians hold it in great esteem for stomach ailments and to aid digestion. They also use it for the altitude sickness that affects those who climb to the snow-covered peaks, which causes great fatigue and fainting. It is also said to strengthen bones and is a remedy for those with soft bones.
cobo.preparation: Leaves brewed as tea; sometimes combined with other highland herbs
cobo.condition: Altitude sickness, digestive complaints, bone weakness (possibly osteoporosis)
cobo.notes: The bone-strengthening claim is particularly interesting — osteoporosis was not a recognized condition in 16th century European medicine. Cobo is documenting an indigenous clinical observation.

modern_research.pubmed_summary: Limited studies. Some evidence for antimicrobial activity. Traditional use for altitude sickness supported by anecdotal reports. Calcium content may support traditional bone-health claims. Needs systematic investigation.
modern_research.significance: HIGH PRIORITY — altitude medicine application for a condition affecting millions globally. Bone health application essentially uninvestigated.

cobo.original_spanish: En los punas más altas y frías, donde no se da maíz ni otras plantas por el excesivo frío, crece la maca, que es como nabo pequeño con hojas tendidas por el suelo. Críase a cuatro mil varas y más sobre el mar, donde parece imposible que cosa viva subsista. Los indios la comen cocida y dicen que da mucha fuerza y calor al cuerpo, y que hace fértiles a las mujeres que de suyo son estériles. Los españoles que la han probado dicen también que les da vigor y que es muy sustentadora.
cobo.translation: In the highest and coldest punas, where maize and other plants do not grow due to excessive cold, maca grows — it is like a small turnip with leaves spread across the ground. It grows at four thousand varas and more above the sea, where it seems impossible that any living thing could subsist. The Indians eat it cooked and say it gives great strength and warmth to the body, and makes fertile those women who are by nature sterile. Spaniards who have tried it also say it gives them vigor and is very sustaining.
cobo.preparation: Root eaten cooked or dried; powdered form also used
cobo.condition: Infertility, fatigue, cold adaptation, general vigor
cobo.notes: Cobo documents both indigenous and Spanish use — a rare cross-cultural validation within the colonial period itself. The altitude specificity (4000+ varas = approximately 3300+ meters) is botanically precise.

modern_research.pubmed_summary: Multiple RCTs showing improved sexual function in men and women. Some evidence for energy and mood. Mechanisms not fully understood. Growing pharmaceutical interest. Now sold globally as supplement.
modern_research.significance: One of the better-researched Andean plants. Commercial interest has driven research. Traditional fertility and energy claims substantially validated.

badianus.latin_original: Ad morbum gallicum: Herbae ololiuhqui semen, atque oleum ex eo expressum, cum lapide tizatl et alba terra mixtum, atque calefactum, corpori illiniatur. Dolor statim sedat.
badianus.translation: For the French disease [syphilis]: The seed of the herb ololiuhqui, and the oil pressed from it, mixed with the stone tizatl and white earth, heated and applied to the body. Pain is immediately calmed.
badianus.preparation: Seeds ground, oil extracted, mixed with mineral compounds and applied topically when heated
badianus.notes: The indigenous physician Martín de la Cruz specifically documents this for syphilis — a disease likely introduced by Europeans. This suggests active pharmacological experimentation in the post-conquest period.

florentine.nahuatl_original: In ololiuhqui: tlanelhuayo, tlazo, achi patlahuac, achi huiac. Ic tlapaloa, ic patia, ic tlachipahua in yollotli.
florentine.translation: The ololiuhqui: it has roots, it is precious, somewhat broad, somewhat long. With it one gives courage, with it one is healed, with it the heart is purified.
florentine.notes: The Florentine Codex emphasizes the psychological and ritual dimension that the Badianus focuses on pharmacologically. Together they document both the physical and psychological therapeutic applications.

modern_research.key_compound: Ergine (d-lysergic acid amide, LSA) — related to LSD
modern_research.pubmed_summary: Albert Hofmann (LSD discoverer) identified ergine in ololiuqui seeds 1960. Psychoactive at threshold doses. Anti-inflammatory properties of alkaloid fraction under investigation. Traditional pain management use may have pharmacological basis beyond psychoactive effects.
modern_research.significance: Cross-source validation between Badianus and Florentine Codex. The pain management application may have a pharmacological basis in the ergine alkaloids that is distinct from psychoactive effects.

florentine.nahuatl_original: Teonanácatl: xitomatl iuhqui, tlapaltic, ixtomio. Amo qualoni in zan iyac. Teyolquima, teixco motta, tenahuatia.
florentine.translation: The divine mushroom: it is like a tomato, colored, its face covered. It is not good to eat for its flavor alone. It intoxicates, it makes one see visions, it speaks to one.
florentine.notes: Sahagún documents both the phenomenology and the therapeutic context. The Nahuatl word teonanácatl means 'divine flesh' or 'flesh of the gods.' The Florentine Codex preserves this knowledge despite missionary hostility to its use.

modern_research.pubmed_summary: FDA Breakthrough Therapy designation for psilocybin in treatment-resistant depression (2018, 2019). Multiple Phase 2/3 trials showing remarkable efficacy. Imperial College London, Johns Hopkins, NYU studies showing 60-80% response rates in treatment-resistant cases. May be most significant psychiatric drug development in 50 years.
modern_research.significance: The Florentine Codex documents clinical use of psilocybin mushrooms 450 years before contemporary psychiatry rediscovered their therapeutic potential. The indigenous therapeutic framework — guided ceremonial context, specific dosing, integration practice — mirrors what modern clinical trials have found most effective.

badianus.latin_original: Ad febres: Herbae yauhtli folia contrita, cum aqua calida misce, et bibe. Etiam ad dolores ventris et ad spiritum difficilem valet.
badianus.translation: For fevers: The crushed leaves of the herb yauhtli, mixed with warm water, drink. It is also effective for belly pains and difficult breathing.
badianus.preparation: Leaves crushed and steeped in warm water as tea
badianus.notes: The Badianus documents yauhtli for three distinct therapeutic applications — a broad-spectrum utility plant.

florentine.nahuatl_original: Yauhtli: xochitl in ixquich itlapal. Tlapaltic, tlazohtli, achi tomahhuac. Temauhcaittani in tlaveliloc. Ic tlapachohua, ic nehmacehua.
florentine.translation: Yauhtli: a flower of all colors. Colored, precious, somewhat thick. It makes the wicked fearful. With it one covers oneself, with it one does penance.
florentine.notes: The Florentine Codex emphasizes the ritual protective dimension alongside the medicinal. The convergence with the Badianus on fever treatment specifically is a strong validation signal.

modern_research.pubmed_summary: Tagetes lucida shows antimicrobial, antifungal, and antioxidant activity in multiple studies. Thymol has well-documented antimicrobial properties. Anti-inflammatory effects consistent with fever-reduction use. Some evidence for mild psychoactive/anxiolytic effects potentially explaining ritual use.
modern_research.significance: CONVERGENCE: Both Badianus (indigenous physician's practice) and Florentine Codex (ethnographic documentation) independently confirm fever and digestive applications. Strong cross-source validation.

cobo.original_spanish: La contrayerba es planta de grande virtud y muy estimada de los indios del Perú y Nueva España. Llámase así porque es contraria a los venenos y ponzoñas, y de ella usan los indios para remediar las mordeduras de víboras y culebras y para todos los venenos en general. Es planta de hojas anchas y raíz grande y olorosa. Dada a beber con agua o mascada y puesta sobre la herida, extrae el veneno con notable presteza.
cobo.translation: The contrayerba is a plant of great virtue and highly esteemed by the Indians of Peru and New Spain. It is so named because it is contrary to venoms and poisons, and the Indians use it to remedy the bites of vipers and snakes and for all poisons in general. It is a plant with broad leaves and a large, fragrant root. Given to drink with water, or chewed and placed upon the wound, it extracts the venom with notable speed.
cobo.preparation: Root chewed and applied topically to bite; also decocted in water as oral antidote
cobo.notes: The cross-regional distribution (both Peru and New Spain) documented by Cobo is itself significant — suggests the anti-venom application was validated across independent traditions.

modern_research.pubmed_summary: Very limited modern research despite extensive traditional use across two continents. Some preliminary evidence for anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial activity. Anti-venom properties essentially uninvestigated with modern methods.
modern_research.significance: HIGH PRIORITY GAP — documented anti-venom use across two independent traditions (Peru and New Spain) with essentially zero modern pharmacological investigation. Snakebite kills 100,000+ people annually globally.

badianus.latin_original: Ad tumores et dolores articulorum: Herbae toloatzin folia in oleo cocta et calida parti affectae applicentur. Statim dolorem sedat et tumorem reducit. Cave ne nimis utaris: venenum est si plus quam par est sumitur.
badianus.translation: For swellings and joint pains: The leaves of the herb toloatzin, cooked in oil and applied warm to the affected part. It immediately calms pain and reduces swelling. Take care not to use too much: it is a poison if taken in more than the proper amount.
badianus.preparation: Leaves cooked in oil, applied warm topically
badianus.condition: Joint pain, swelling (possibly gout or arthritis), topical pain management
badianus.notes: The Badianus physician's dosage warning is remarkably sophisticated — demonstrating understanding of the therapeutic window, a concept not formalized in Western medicine for centuries.

florentine.nahuatl_original: Toloatzin: xochitl, patli. Teixco motta, teyolloquima. Zan quezqui ic patia: miac ic miqui.
florentine.translation: Toloatzin: flower, medicine. It causes visions, it intoxicates. Only a little heals: too much causes death.
florentine.notes: The Florentine Codex's warning exactly mirrors the Badianus — 'only a little heals, too much causes death.' This identical dosage caveat across two independent sources is a powerful validation of sophisticated pharmacological understanding.

modern_research.pubmed_summary: Scopolamine: FDA-approved for motion sickness, post-operative nausea. Atropine: WHO Essential Medicine, used in surgery, cardiac arrest, nerve agent treatment. Hyoscyamine: used for GI conditions. All three alkaloids are active pharmaceuticals. Topical Datura preparations for joint pain have documented anti-inflammatory mechanisms.
modern_research.significance: EXCEPTIONAL CONVERGENCE: Both Badianus and Florentine Codex independently document identical therapeutic use AND identical dosage warning. Both sources contain more sophisticated pharmacological understanding than most contemporaneous European medical texts. Active compounds are now WHO Essential Medicines.

cobo.original_spanish: El paico o hierba santa se halla en todas partes de este reino, en los valles calientes y en las sierras templadas. Los indios la usan grandemente para las lombrices de los niños y de los adultos, dándola a beber cocida en agua o en la chicha. También sirve para los males del estómago y para los que escupen sangre. Es yerba de hoja menuda y muy olorosa.
cobo.translation: The paico or holy herb is found in all parts of this kingdom, in the warm valleys and in the temperate mountains. The Indians use it greatly for worms in children and adults, giving it to drink cooked in water or in chicha. It also serves for stomach ailments and for those who spit blood. It is an herb with small leaves and very fragrant.
cobo.preparation: Leaves boiled in water or fermented chicha; drunk as decoction
cobo.condition: Intestinal parasites, digestive disorders, respiratory bleeding
cobo.notes: Cobo documents use from valley to highland — unusually broad geographic distribution within Peru, suggesting widely validated efficacy.

badianus.latin_original: Ad vermes intestinorum: Herbae epazotl sucum cum aqua tepida bibe. Vermes expellit cito. Etiam ad stomachi dolores et ad febres prodest.
badianus.translation: For intestinal worms: Drink the juice of the herb epazotl with warm water. It expels worms quickly. It is also beneficial for stomach pains and fevers.
badianus.preparation: Fresh juice extracted and drunk with warm water
badianus.notes: The Badianus physician documents the same antiparasitic use as Cobo, 3000 miles distant, across independent traditions.

florentine.nahuatl_original: Epazotl: patli, qualli patli. Ic quixtia in coatl, in cueitl. Ixnextia, tlapaltic.
florentine.translation: Epazotl: medicine, good medicine. With it the worm and the skirt-worm are expelled. It cleanses, it is potent.
florentine.notes: Three terms for worms in the Nahuatl text suggest a sophisticated classification of intestinal parasites. The Florentine Codex corroborates both the Badianus and Cobo on antiparasitic use.

modern_research.pubmed_summary: Ascaridole has well-documented anthelmintic (antiparasitic) activity. Used as pharmaceutical anthelmintic through mid-20th century. Some resistance to modern antiparasitic drugs has renewed interest. Active compounds also show antimicrobial and antifungal activity. Widely used in traditional medicine across 3 continents.
modern_research.significance: MAXIMUM CONVERGENCE: All three sources — Cobo (Andean), Badianus (Aztec physician), Florentine Codex (Aztec ethnography) — independently document identical antiparasitic application across two continents. Fully validated pharmacologically. Renewed interest as drug-resistant parasites emerge globally.

sources.cobo: Historia del Nuevo Mundo, Bernabé Cobo SJ, 1653. Books 4-9.
sources.badianus: Libellus de Medicinalibus Indorum Herbis (Badianus Manuscript), Martín de la Cruz / Juan de Badiano, 1552.
sources.florentine: Historia General de las Cosas de Nueva España (Florentine Codex), Bernardino de Sahagún, 1577. Book 11.

metadata.convergence_methodology: Plants are scored 1-10 based on: number of independent sources confirming use (0-4 points), cross-continental validation (0-2 points), consistency of preparation and condition described (0-2 points), modern pharmacological validation (0-2 points).
